name: Cortals d'Encamp and Tosa de Llosada Loop
name_original: Camí Ral y Tosa de Llosada desde Prats — circular por Camí de Cortals y Estany Primer de Pessons
description: This difficult 22.3-mile mountain bike route from Prats explores the Camí Ral, Tosa de Llosada, and the scenic Estany Primer de Pessons in A
region_intro: Dog friendly hiking trails in the Pyrenees traverse a diverse mountain range forming a natural border between France and Spain, stretching over 430 km. The region is characterized by rugged peaks, deep glacial valleys, and extensive forests, transitioning from Atlantic-influenced landscapes in the west to Mediterranean climates in the east. Hikers can explore varied terrains including alpine meadows, granite massifs, and areas with numerous glacial lakes and waterfalls. Best dog friendly hiking trails in the Pyrenees The most popular dog friendly hiking route is Cola de Caballo via the Soaso Steps from the Ordesa Meadow, a 11.1 miles (17.8 km) trail that takes 5 hours 18 minutes to complete. This difficult route leads to the impressive Cola de Caballo waterfall within the Ordesa Valley. Another top favourite among local hikers is Ibones d'Ordicuso – semi-circular from Baños de Panticosa, a moderate 2.9 miles (4.6 km) path. This route offers views of glacial lakes (ibones) and surrounding peaks. Local hikers also love the Iron Route Trail, a 4.0 miles (6.5 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 52 minutes. Hiking in the Pyrenees is defined by dramatic peaks, glacial lakes, and dense woodlands, offering a wide range of options for different ability levels. The routes in Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 38,000 reviews. More than 340,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Pyrenees's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many dog-friendly hiking trails are available in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees offer a vast network of trails suitable for hiking with your dog. You can explore over 180 dog-friendly routes, ranging from easy strolls to more challenging mountain expeditions.

Q: Are there easy dog-friendly hikes suitable for beginners or older dogs?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees cater to all levels. There are 28 easy dog-friendly routes perfect for beginners, families, or dogs who prefer a more relaxed pace. These trails often feature gentler terrain and shorter distances.

Q: Can I find dog-friendly circular routes in the Pyrenees?
A: Absolutely. Many dog-friendly trails in the Pyrenees are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point without retracing your steps. For example, consider the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1218906465/ascent-to-the-ibones-d-ordicuso-circular-by-the-cascada-de-argualas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Ibones d'Ordicuso — circular by the Cascada de Argualas&lt;/a&gt;, which offers stunning views and a refreshing waterfall.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly trails that feature waterfalls?
A: The Pyrenees are renowned for their spectacular waterfalls. You can find dog-friendly trails that lead to impressive cascades, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10530225" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beech Forest – Cave Waterfall loop from Rio Arazas&lt;/a&gt;. The region's glacial valleys often hide beautiful waterfalls, providing a refreshing stop for you and your dog.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on dog-friendly hikes in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees offer incredibly diverse landscapes. You and your dog can explore everything from dense pine and beech forests to open alpine meadows, rugged peaks, and serene glacial valleys. The region transitions from the Atlantic coast to the Mediterranean, offering varied scenery throughout.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking with my dog in the Pyrenees?
A: The best time for dog-friendly hiking in the Pyrenees is typically from late spring to early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is generally milder, and most trails are free of snow. Summer offers lush greenery and warmer temperatures, while spring and autumn provide cooler weather and vibrant foliage.

Q: Are there specific rules or considerations for hiking with dogs in Pyrenees National Parks?
A: Yes, when hiking with your dog in protected areas like the Pyrenees National Park (France) or Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park (Spain), it's crucial to be aware of specific regulations. Dogs are often required to be on a leash, and in some sensitive zones, they might not be permitted at all to protect wildlife. Always check the specific park's rules before your visit.

Q: What do other hikers say about dog-friendly trails in the Pyrenees?
A: The dog-friendly trails in the Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars. Reviewers often praise the breathtaking natural beauty, the variety of terrain, and the opportunity to share these incredible experiences with their canine companions.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly trails that lead to lakes or offer swimming opportunities?
A: The Pyrenees are dotted with numerous glacial lakes, known as "ibones" in the Aragonese Pyrenees. Many trails lead to these beautiful bodies of water, offering a chance for your dog to cool off. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e937749845/ibones-d-ordicuso-semi-circular-from-banos-de-panticosa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibones d'Ordicuso – semi-circular from Baños de Panticosa&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that takes you to stunning mountain lakes. The famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5186545" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lac d'Oô&lt;/a&gt; is another popular spot.

Q: Are there any easy, short dog-friendly walks in the Pyrenees?
A: For a shorter, easier walk with your dog, the Pyrenees offer several options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1389836371/iron-route-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Route Trail&lt;/a&gt; is an easy hike with minimal elevation gain, perfect for a leisurely outing. These types of trails are ideal for a quick stretch or for dogs with less stamina.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions I can visit with my dog in the Pyrenees?
A: Beyond the trails themselves, the Pyrenees boast many natural wonders. While some areas like the core of Cirque de Gavarnie might have restrictions, you can often find dog-friendly paths leading to viewpoints or surrounding areas. The region is rich in glacial formations, dramatic peaks, and diverse ecosystems, offering plenty to explore with your dog.
tips: An incredible trail runs along the lower slopes of the valley between Canillo and Prats. The trail is surrounded by a beautiful pine forest.
Impressive viewpoint that if you're running through the area it's worth getting there and taking a look at the views it offers together with the hermitage of Sant Felip and Sant Jaume dels Cortals.
A mountain pass and one of the intermediate stops of the ski lift. You can use it to go downhill from here. Before you do, stop and admire the incredible views.

An incredible gravel track through the Corteles Valley. The angled path reaches the Cortales peak at 2500 meters above sea level. A multi-use track through this Luch valley that can be used in both directions.
The Estany Primer de Pessons is a beautiful mountain lake that is joined by other high-altitude ponds by the Pessons River. A great place to stop if you need to rest.
Gravel trails above El Peretol, rising above the lush valley towards the ski resort. Great place for summer adventures.
At 2560 meters above sea level, the summit of the Llosada peak in the Grandvalira ski resort. Great gravel paths around this high altitude plateau.
It is one of the highest mountains that remain at the top of the Grandvalira Sector de Canillo station and that has good views of the valleys that open up in the north of the Andorran country.
Route that connects Pret and the Grandvalira ski resort above Canillo, with multiple gravel slopes to explore in season when the snow disappears. Nice little pond in this natural mountain depression.

faqs.question: Is this mountain biking trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is rated as difficult and is not suitable for beginners. It requires very good fitness and advanced mountain biking skills. Riders should expect technical sections, potentially necessitating dismounting and pushing their bikes, especially given that red trails in Andorra can feel more like black in terms of technical demands.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this mountain biking route?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this route is varied and highly technical, characteristic of high-mountain trails in the Pyrenees. Expect rocky and uneven paths, narrow sections, obstacles like branches and stones, and significant gradients. Some parts, especially around Estany Primer de Pessons, will be particularly challenging.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this mountain biking loop?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this ~35.8 km mountain biking loop is approximately 4 hours and 46 minutes. However, this can vary significantly based on your fitness level, technical skill, and how often you stop to enjoy the views or navigate challenging sections.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers breathtaking high-alpine scenery. Key highlights include the stunning Estany Primer de Pessons, a beautiful high-altitude lake, and the expansive views from Tosa de Llosada and Sant Felip i Sant Jaume dels Cortals Viewpoint. You'll also pass by the unique Rock Arch on Camí Ral and other natural features like Pla de les Pedres.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to mountain bike this high-altitude trail is typically during the summer and early autumn months, from late June to September. This period ensures that most of the snow has melted, and the weather conditions are generally more stable and favorable for mountain biking in the Pyrenees.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trailhead for this route in Prats?
faqs.answer: Specific parking details for the trailhead in Prats are not provided, but Prats is a small village in Andorra. It's advisable to look for designated parking areas within Prats or near the start of the Camí Ral. Public transport options to Prats may also be available, connecting from larger towns in Andorra.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this mountain biking trail?
faqs.answer: While Andorra generally welcomes dogs on trails, specific regulations for this particular route are not detailed. Given it's a challenging mountain biking trail in a high-alpine environment, it's recommended to keep dogs on a leash for their safety and to protect local wildlife. Always ensure your dog is fit enough for such a demanding ride.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to ride this trail in Grandvalira?
faqs.answer: This trail is located in the Grandvalira area. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for mountain biking on natural trails in Andorra. However, it's always good practice to check the latest regulations on the official Visit Andorra website or local tourism offices, especially if you plan to use any ski lift infrastructure that might be open for bikes.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this circular route incorporates sections of several other trails. You will ride along parts of the [CIMA AN05] Cortals d'Emcamp, GRP La volta a Andorra, Camí de l'Ossa, El Camí IT1TR5 El Camí Capçaler: de Salzes a Balaguer, and various segments of the Camí del Canal and Cami Ral.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restaurants near the trail, especially around Estany Primer de Pessons or Prats?
faqs.answer: While the high-alpine sections, particularly around Estany Primer de Pessons, are remote and lack facilities, you may find cafes, pubs, and restaurants in the village of Prats at the start/end of the route. For options closer to the Els Cortals d'Encamp area, you might find services, especially during peak seasons.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a mountain bike ride on this challenging trail?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty and high-alpine environment, you should bring appropriate gear. This includes a well-maintained mountain bike suitable for technical terrain, a helmet, protective pads, plenty of water and snacks, a repair kit, first-aid supplies, navigation tools (like a GPS device or the komoot app), and layers of clothing to adapt to changing mountain weather conditions.
